message:
fix(evaluator): per-segment safe-pattern eval prevents compound-command bypass

Closes a real bypass in non-core packs (everything outside
`core.filesystem`): a safe pattern matching anywhere in a compound
command would skip the entire pack's destructive evaluation, even
when the destructive activity was in a *different* segment of the
compound.

Concretely, before this fix:

    railway service list && railway volume delete --volume prod-db --yes

…would slip past `platform.railway` because:

1. `pack.matches_safe_with_deadline(command_for_packs, deadline)`
   was called against the *whole* compound. The Railway pack's
   safe pattern for `railway service list` matched (correctly —
   that subcommand is read-only).
2. Matching the safe pattern caused the entire pack's destructive
   evaluation to be skipped via `continue`, so the
   `railway-volume-delete` regex was never run against the
   second segment.
3. The command was reported as allowed.

This affected every non-core pack that combines safe and
destructive predicates. The exact same shape works for any
destructive operator + an inert command on either side (` ; ` /
` || ` / ` & ` / ` && `). The original commit that introduced
single-pass per-pack evaluation specifically called out this case
in a comment ("safe patterns only protect their own pack's
destructive patterns, not other packs") but didn't extend the
protection across segments inside the same pack.

Fix:

1. **Compute segment ranges up front** via the existing
   `crate::packs::split_command_segments(cmd)`. The new helper
   `command_segment_ranges(cmd)` returns each segment's
   `(start, end)` byte offsets inside `command_for_packs` —
   computed by pointer arithmetic against the slice's `as_ptr()`,
   so we don't double-allocate the segment strings.
   `has_compound_segments = segment_ranges.len() > 1` is the
   new gate.

2. **Per-segment safe + destructive evaluation in the non-core
   branch.** When `has_compound_segments` is true, iterate each
   segment range and run safe+destructive against just that
   segment's slice. A safe match on segment N now only excuses
   segment N — segment N+1 still gets full destructive evaluation.
   Single-segment commands fall through to the existing path
   unchanged (one iteration, no per-segment dispatch overhead).

3. **Skip duplicate per-pack destructive scan when segment
   evaluation already ran.** After the segment loop, the existing
   "global" destructive scan inside this function would normally
   still run and could re-flag the same matches. Added a guard:
   `span_is_inside_any_segment(span, &segment_ranges)` skips spans
   already covered by the per-segment scan. The check is `O(N)`
   over segments (N ≤ a few in practice) so it's negligible.

4. **Extracted `evaluate_pack_destructive_patterns` helper.** The
   per-segment loop and the existing single-pass loop need the
   same destructive-pattern matching logic (allowlist lookup,
   span mapping, severity propagation, suggestion forwarding).
   Pulled the body into a helper that takes the slice, the slice
   offset for span remapping, and all the surrounding context.
   The helper is `#[allow(clippy::too_many_arguments)]` because
   bundling the args into a struct here would mostly add noise —
   eight references with no shared lifetime invariant.

   Span mapping inside the helper: spans returned by the regex
   are relative to `command_slice`, so we add `slice_offset`
   before calling `map_span_with_offset`. The
   `extract_match_preview` fallback uses `slice_span =
   span - slice_offset` so the preview is still extracted from
   `command_slice` and not from the original concatenated
   command (which would produce off-by-one previews on later
   segments).

5. **Three new tests** covering the bypass case symmetrically:
   - `non_core_safe_segment_does_not_mask_later_destructive_
     segment` — `safe && destructive`. Was the original bypass.
   - `non_core_safe_segment_does_not_mask_earlier_destructive_
     segment` — `destructive && safe`. Mirror case to make sure
     the destructive scan also covers leading segments.
   - `non_core_safe_segments_remain_allowed` — sanity check that
     two genuinely-safe segments still pass; we didn't want to
     fix the bypass by simply blocking everything compound.

   New `evaluate_with_pack_ids(command, &["platform.railway"])`
   helper builds the same enabled-pack registry shape the
   non-test code uses, then calls
   `evaluate_command_with_pack_order(...)`. Avoids leaking
   private function arguments into the test signatures.

`core.filesystem` is unchanged — that pack is already
segment-aware via its own custom path inside the upper branch
of the same function.

Behavior change notes:

- Commands that legitimately combine a safe operation with a
  reasonable destructive one (e.g. an aliased
  `railway service list && rm -rf /tmp/junk`) will now be
  blocked on the destructive segment — that's the correct
  behavior. Users running this kind of compound deliberately
  should either run the segments separately or add an allowlist
  rule for the destructive part.
- No new false positives expected: the safe-pattern check still
  runs per segment and still excuses segments that match it.

Performance: the per-segment loop runs only when the command has
more than one segment (a small minority of commands). Within the
loop, the existing pattern compilation and deadline checks are
unchanged. Overall expected overhead is negligible on the typical
single-command path and bounded by O(segments × patterns) on
compound commands, which is the cost we always should have been
paying for correct evaluation.
